content.full_text:
February 24, 1977

Joseph E. B. Gaudet  
Watson and Hoffe  
3700 Barrett Avenue  
Richmond, CA 94605

Dear Mr. Gaudet:

You called yesterday to ask about the reporting obligations of your client, Judge Coleman F. Fannin. You indicated that Judge Fannin is a former member of your law firm and that when he left your firm to become a judge, he entered into a termination agreement with his former partners. As part of the agreement, Judge Fannin is entitled to receive a share of future fees received from specifically designated clients for work performed while Judge Fannin was an active partner. You indicated that pursuant to this agreement, Judge Fannin received approximately $19,000 from a single client during the pertinent reporting period and you asked how this should be reported.

Under Government Code Sections 87203 and 87207(a), Judge Fannin is required to disclose all sources of income aggregating $250 or more. The source of income in this case is the client, not the law firm. The law firm is only serving as a conduit for the funds. We base this conclusion on our understanding that Judge Fannin has no recourse against the law firm should the client fail to pay and has a right to income only if the client pays the fees due.

You also indicated that under the agreement with his former partners, Judge Fannin has a right to share in fees that may be received from other former clients. You asked whether this constitutes a reportable investment. Government Code Section 82034 defines an "investment" as "any financial interest in ... a business entity ..." A "business entity" is defined in Section 82005 as "any organization or enterprise operated for profit ..." The arrangement you describe between Judge Fannin and his former partners is not in the nature of a "business entity" since it is not an active enterprise operated for profit but instead is only a contract under which Judge Fannin has certain rights. Accordingly, his right to receive future income under this agreement is not a reportable investment.

Finally, you indicated in a second telephone conversation today that Judge Fannin has a security interest in certain real property by virtue of a letter agreement between himself and his former partners. You indicated that the interest is in the nature of a second deed of trust, although it has not been recorded as such. Such an interest is reportable as an interest in real property. The value of the interest is the amount owed to Judge Fannin. It is permissible for Judge Fannin to include an explanation on his disclosure statement of the particulars of this security interest for purposes of clarification.

If I can be of any further assistance to you, please let me know.

Sincerely yours,

Michael J. Baker
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

MJB:plh
